findings.title: The share card asserts the authenticity the endnote disclaims — and the caveat is not where readers form conclusions
findings.target: dialect-differences/index.html og:description line 13 ("The same clinical phrases, framed the way different communities actually speak") vs. the page endnote line 307 ("an LLM's version of a dialect is an approximation and may miss how a community actually speaks"); homepage Fig. 3 specimen headline "Same medical situation. Different dialect. Different prediction." (index.html line 381)
findings.argument: Readers form conclusions in three places: the social-share preview, the homepage specimen, and the matrix at the top of the dialect page. The first affirmatively claims community authenticity ("the way different communities actually speak"), the second names "dialect" as the cause with no authorship disclosure anywhere on the homepage (the specimen note lists graph counts and batch date only), and the third is captioned as framings "replac[ing] standard English" with the LLM-approximation caveat deferred to small muted mono text at the very bottom of the page. The og:description is not a caveat weakened by placement — it is the opposite claim. Every conclusion-forming surface says 'dialect evidence'; only the footer says 'LLM probe'.
findings.fix: Rewrite the og/twitter descriptions to "LLM-approximated dialect and register framings" (they are metadata, not measured numbers, so this is a one-line edit consistent with the provenance-label hard rule); add one sentence of the endnote's approximation language to the dialect page explainer above the matrix and to the homepage specimen's spec-note string; retitle the homepage specimen "Different framing. Different prediction."

findings.title: Named varieties (AAVE, Caribbean, ESL) are measured by one unvalidated LLM sentence per cell, and the 'Caribbean English' column is not one variety
findings.target: data/dialects.json variant prompts and the matrix column headers produced by shortName() in dialect-differences/index.html (lines 333-341)
findings.argument: Column-level numbers are attributed to named, racialized and stigmatized varieties on the basis of a single Claude-written sentence per term, screened by validators that check term fixity, not dialectal accuracy. The 'Caribbean English' column mixes Jamaican Creole basilect ("Mi clinically depressed fi true, so mi haffi take a"; "Fi manage di HIV, mi haffi tek mi" — a distinct language variety, not a dialect of English) with mesolectal/acrolectal rows ("I does grab my inhaler" — Trinidadian-style habitual does; "I going to just go over to"), so its per-column deltas average across different points on a creole continuum from different territories. 'ESL-influenced English' is not a variety at all, and its probes are a stereotype of one L1 group ("I am needing to take my", "I am very... so I must"). Several 'AAVE' cells are generic colloquialism with an ethnic label ("I'm straight up... I know I gotta"). Reporting per-community effect numbers from these probes is exactly the measurement-without-representation the field warns against; the site's own roadmap (start-here step 7: "next: community-validated dialects") concedes validation hasn't happened.
findings.fix: Until community/linguist validation exists, relabel matrix columns as generator instructions, not varieties (e.g. "styled: AAVE (LLM instruction)"), split or drop the Caribbean column (its rows are not commensurable), rename ESL to "L2-influenced (stereotyped probe)" or cut it, and state per-cell n=1 in the matrix caption. Gate any return of community names on the already-promised validation step.

findings.title: Per-cell dialect deltas sit inside the study's own paraphrase-noise floor, which is published in a data file but surfaced on no page — and the featured 'flips' run toward more clinical answers
findings.target: dialect-differences matrix ("Change in p(target), by term and framing") and homepage specimen red flip marks, vs. data/provenance.json paraphrase_robustness (mean_abs_delta_vs_baseline = 0.0642, verdict: "single pairs are illustrations, inference lives in the paired aggregate")
findings.argument: The engine's own control found innocuous meaning-preserving rewording moves these single-sentence probabilities by mean |Δ| ≈ 0.064 — the magnitude of most matrix cells (AAVE +0.09, Southern +0.055, Caribbean −0.038). That verdict string exists only in provenance.json; grep shows 'paraphrase' never appears on the dialect page, homepage, or start-here. Without the benchmark, readers read noise-scale cells as dialect effects. Worse, the data contradict a dialect-penalty reading: AAVE never flips the top prediction in any of the 5 terms and has positive deltas in 3, and the featured specimen's penalty-red flips land on 'pill' and 'medication' against a baseline target of 'break' — more medical, not less. Matrix flips also count 'medication'→'meds' as a top-pick flip. The presentation borrows the language-penalty visual grammar (red = flip = risk) for data that show no directional dialect harm.
findings.fix: Print the 0.064 paraphrase-noise benchmark in the matrix caption ("innocuous rewording moves these numbers by ±0.06 on average; cells near that are noise"); color flips by direction relative to the clinical target (toward-clinical vs. away) instead of undifferentiated penalty red; exclude wordform-variant flips or footnote them; state explicitly that no per-variety effect is claimed at n=5 terms, or compute per-variety aggregates with CIs before implying one.

findings.title: 'Standard English' baseline, 'prestige form', and unlabeled AAVE morphosyntax as generic 'nonstandard grammar'
findings.target: dialect-differences featured table baseline row labeled "Standard English" (JS line 487) over the baseline sentence "I'm totally clinically depressed, so I should take a"; wording-differences 2×2 labels "A: prestige form" / "Nonstandard morphosyntax" (lines 224-232) with exemplar "I been having insomnia... I'ma finally call"; homepage generalization "From our analysis, changing the grammar alone shifts +11–13%" (index.html line 368)
findings.argument: Three compounding problems. (1) The comparison baseline labeled "Standard English" is itself colloquial ("I'm totally clinically depressed"), so every delta measures distance from an arbitrary casual sentence while the caption claims framings "replace standard English" — a standard-language-ideology default the data don't instantiate. (2) The 'nonstandard' quadrant is built from identifiably AAVE-associated morphosyntax (unstressed been + I'ma) presented namelessly as generic bad grammar opposed to a cell called "prestige form" — 'prestige' is a claim about social evaluation, not structure, and the pairing quietly encodes AAVE-features-degrade-the-model as a deficit finding while hiding which variety was tested. (3) The homepage states the +11–13% grammar effect as a general result ("From our analysis..."), but it is one simulated insomnia item, single trace, and the companion register Δ (+4–5%) is below the study's own 0.064 paraphrase-noise floor.
findings.fix: Relabel the baseline row "generation baseline (colloquial)"; rename the 2×2 cells descriptively ("standardized written register" / name the actual features tested: "AAVE-associated morphosyntax: remote/perfect been, I'ma") and drop "prestige form"; on the homepage, scope the Fig. 2 claim to "in this one traced case" and delete "From our analysis", and annotate the register Δ as within paraphrase noise.

findings.title: Three of five dialect-matrix rows measure function-word targets, so most 'dialect effect' cells measure syntactic frame, not medical interpretation
findings.target: data/dialects.json rows 'inhaler' (target "my"), 'postprandial' (target "the"), 'having syncopal episodes' (target "the"), feeding the dialect page's headline counts ("5 clinical terms · 9 framing varieties · N re-traced sentences · N top-pick flips") and the claim "any shift comes from framing"
findings.argument: p("the") after "go on over to" vs. "go to", or p("my") after "I be grabbing my inhaler and", tracks the rewritten verb frame's syntactic expectations, which the dialect rewrite changes by construction — Southern US raising p("the") from 0.315 to 0.578 says nothing about how the model handles a Southern speaker's medical meaning. Yet these cells are majority contributors to the matrix, its flip count, and the page's counts under a heading about clinical terms. The 'postprandial' row compounds this: "Now that I have the postprandial" is not well-formed clinical English (postprandial is an adjective), so its baseline isn't a clinical register anchor at all. Under the page's framing ("Change in p(target)" for clinical terms), over half the displayed evidence is measuring the wrong construct.
findings.fix: Either restrict the matrix to rows whose target is a clinically contentful continuation (the 'HIV'→'medication' pattern) or add a visible target-class tag per row ("function-word target: measures syntax, not care content") and exclude function-word rows from the flip/summary counts; retire or regenerate the 'postprandial' row.

findings.title: 'The wording chooses the answer' is anchored on a single-case ladder the study's own data file calls unrepresentative
findings.target: start-here/index.html line 189 caption "the wording chooses the answer — measured on gemma-2-2b" linking the dyspepsia ladder, vs. data/provenance.json ladder_n10 verdict ("no reliable register gradient with the clinical term held fixed; the single-case staircase (ladder_digestive) is real but not representative")
findings.argument: Start-here's step-2 figure (antacid / ice cream / apple) plus the dyspepsia-ladder link teaches new readers that casualness per se steers the model. The n=10 replication found rung means flat (0.27–0.34), and that null is disclosed only inside a collapsed <details> fold on the dialect page and in a JSON verdict string — never on start-here, where the generalization is made. The defensible claim (lexical term swaps carry the aggregate penalty; register alone does not) is actually more interesting than the one displayed, and 'wording chooses the answer' additionally over-generalizes synthetic single-token probes on a 2B base model, which the methods limitations acknowledge but start-here never echoes.
findings.fix: Next to the dyspepsia-ladder link add the one-line null ("across 10 baselines the register ladder is flat, 27–34%; the swap of the term itself carries the effect"), change the caption to "the term choice moves the answer — in these synthetic probes on gemma-2-2b", and un-fold the ladder null on the dialect page so it sits beside the single-case staircase it corrects.
